chunks.title: Introduction to Glyphosate
chunks.summary: The segment begins with a viewer's question about glyphosate and whether it should be a concern for human health. The speaker acknowledges a lot of concern surrounding glyphosate and introduces the idea of needing more research to fully understand its effects.
chunks.transcript: okay next question is from Brian who says Paul I've been hearing a lot about glyphosate recently Joe Rogan posted about this there was a study showing that many in the United States have glyphosate in their urine should I worry about glyphosate is there enough evidence for humans to really be concerned about this so glyphosate is Roundup I think there is certainly a lot of concern about glyphosate but what does the evidence really show so when you dig into this what you find is that we probably need

chunks.title: Research and Evidence on Glyphosate
chunks.summary: The discussion focuses on available research regarding glyphosate, highlighting studies that suggest potential harm to human health through its impacts on the gut microbiome and its presence in various consumer products. The speaker expresses personal choices made to limit glyphosate exposure.

chunks.title: Exposure Sources and Personal Precautions
chunks.summary: The speaker lists various everyday products that may contain glyphosate, such as clothing and feminine hygiene products. They also share personal strategies for minimizing exposure, including opting for organic items and being cautious about food sources.
chunks.transcript: course includes buying organic fruit when and where I can I don't eat vegetables and trying to find honey from regions of the world that are not near agriculture or places where there's not glyphosates sprayed on crops nearby I found another article showing that over 60 percent of Honeys were positive for glyphosate I think the numbers probably higher than that but in this article they give a number of interesting pieces of information um glyphosate is transformed into its main metabolite ampa maintains all the toxic characteristics of its precursor it's even more persistent the half-life of ampa is 76 to 240 days basically the takeaway there is that when it's in the environment groundwater it's there for a while I couldn't find any great data on washing it out of clothes I suspect that if you have a shirt or sheets and you wash them I don't know how much glyphosate is going to be stuck in there I can't imagine it's a ton but I at least was cautious and avoided it with my bedding and my clothing many laboratory tests demonstrated the possible absorption of glyphosate in the gastrointestinal tract of humans and mammals as well as absorption through inhalation ingestion and dermal contact hopefully few of you are exposed to glyphosate through dermal contact but it's most of us getting exposed to it through our ingestions the application

chunks.title: Concerns Regarding Food and Water Safety
chunks.summary: The segment discusses findings about glyphosate contamination in honey, food products, and even drinking water, advising listeners on the importance of selecting glyphosate-free options where possible. The speaker suggests using water filtration methods to reduce pesticide contaminants.

chunks.title: Carcinogenic Classification and Glyphosate Risks
chunks.summary: The speaker reviews the history of glyphosate’s carcinogenic classification and its evolving perception in scientific communities. They emphasize the need for caution and further research into its potential long-term effects on human health.
chunks.transcript: previous podcasts to get as much of the pesticide contamination out of my water as possible glyphosate has an interesting history of carcinogenic classification originally classified as a category C substance showing limited evidence of carcinogenicity originally classified or in 1985 classified as a category C substance in 1991 APA changed it to category e substances that do not show carcinogenic potential but in March of 2015 the iarc international Agency for research on cancer in Leon France a department of The Who labeled glyphosate as possibly carcinogenic to humans to a category interestingly that is a category that includes acrylamide red meat bitumen wood combustion fumes Etc I've done previous podcasts and why I don't think red meat should be included in that analysis but take from that what

chunks.title: Animal Studies and Implications for Human Health
chunks.summary: In this segment, a specific study on rats exposed to glyphosate is discussed, revealing concerning results related to non-alcoholic fatty liver disease. The speaker connects these findings to broader health implications for humans and stresses awareness of glyphosate exposure.
chunks.transcript: for the most compelling research suggesting that glyphosate had problematic effects in animal or human models and I came up with one study that I'll show you guys this one is certainly a little bit concerning multi-omics considering multiple metabolic and proteomic disturbances reveal non-alcoholic fatty liver disease in rats following chronic exposure to an ultra low dose of the Roundup herbicide so this is what's interesting for me is that they administered this to the rats for two years which is a long time in Rat years at one at 0.1 parts per billion which is 50 nanograms per liter remember that well two things to consider here a rat is much smaller than a human so we have to adjust this for human size versus rat size but also remember that we were seeing 0.14 milligrams per kilogram which you can basically enter convert kilograms and liters in corn flakes and many of the pastas so doses that are relatively equivalent when we're thinking about body size administered for much of the lives of these rats most of us are probably exposed to glyphosate for most of our lives these doses led to indications of fatty liver across multiple um analyzes in these rats so they say here overall metabolome and proteome disturbances showed a substantial overlap with biomarkers of non-alcoholic fatty liver disease and its progressions to steadohepatosis thus confirm liver functional dysfunction resulting from chronic ultra low glyphosate exposure not a good thing so that's a little scary we certainly know that non-alcoholic fatty liver disease is an epidemic today probably related to insulin resistance and metabolic dysfunction in general but is it possible that low-dose glyphosate exposure in humans could contribute to stiato hepatosis accumulation of fat in the liver and non-alcoholic fatty liver in humans I think it is it's at least something to be aware of and something
